issues.title: intdir Review
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/review-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel-09-intdir-early-matsushima-2025-11-11/:

> Overall this draft was well written and well covered various discard cases,
> TTL expired, exceed MTU size, uRPF, etc., for example.
> 
> Nits:
> - It would be better to clarify which counter type should be used for SR-MPLS
> cases, either invalid-label, or invalid-sid;
> 
> - [RFC8704](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc8704/) might be considered as an informational reference for uRPF;
> 
> - In addition to uRPF, "ingress filtering" in [RFC2827](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc2827/)/3704 would cover more
> wider discard cases at ingress nodes:
> 
> - It would be nice if the draft considered that how many ICMP packets were
> send, or suppressed to the corresponding discard events,
>   such as TTL expired, exceed MTU size, or rejected by policies.

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body: Feedback addressed

issues.title: SECDIR Review (Derrell Piper)
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
Document: draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel
Title: Information and Data Models for Packet Discard Reporting
Reviewer: Derrell Piper
Review result: Ready

I reviewed draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel-13. It defines an information model and YANG data model for packet discard reporting.

I found no blocking security issues. The Security Considerations adequately cover the relevant management-plane concerns, including secure transport, mutual authentication, NACM-based access control, and the sensitivity of readable discard counters.

One non-blocking comment: Section 8.2 identifies the per-interface pdr:traffic and pdr:discards subtrees under if:statistics as sensitive readable data. However, nacm:default-deny-all is applied only to the new control-plane and device discard-stats containers, not to the interface augment into if:statistics. The authors should either reconcile that access-control posture or note why the per-interface breakdown is intentionally left to deployment-specific NACM policy rather than being marked by the module for default-deny-all protection.

Ready.

issues.title: (Carlos Review) ICMP
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 1. No ICMP generation/suppression signal tied to discard events.
>    `ttl-expired`, `mtu-exceeded`, and `policy` discards are
> exactly the
>    conditions RFC 792/RFC 4443 require or permit an ICMP(v6) error
> to
>    accompany. The document is silent on whether the corresponding
> ICMP
>    was sent, suppressed by rate-limiting (RFC 4443 Section 2.4),
> or
>    never generated. This gap matters operationally: traceroute and
> most
>    path-diagnostic OAM depend on these ICMP messages reaching the
>    source, so a discard counter and a traceroute trace of the same
> event
>    can silently diverge -- which incidentaly is exactly the
>    automated-mitigation failure mode the document is meant to
> prevent.
>    RFC 6192 (which can be a useful Informational reference),
> Section 4,
>    documents this as existing operational practice: ICMP error
>    generation, including TTL/Hop-limit-expired handling
> specifically, is
>    commonly rate-limited independently of the forwarding-plane
> discard
>    decision. RFC 4884 (ICMP multi-part messages) is existing
> machinery
>    for carrying diagnostic payload (interface, MPLS label stack, a
> few
>    others) in these same ICMP errors and is worth citing as
>    complementary rather than reinventing it. Recommend either
> modeling
>    ICMP-sent/suppressed state explicitly, or stating the omission
> is a
>    deliberate scope decision with rationale.

[Med] This was a deliberate design choice to focus only on key discard counters and not include a comprehensive list of events. Added a sentence to make this explicit.

issues.title: (Carlos Review) MPLS/SRv6
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 
> 2. Address-family model does not cleanly represent MPLS/SRv6
> traffic,
>    only its discards. The `all` identity functions as a catch-all
>    workaround rather than a proper address-family hierarchy.
> Concretely:
>    `invalid-label`/`invalid-sid` (explicitly, MPLS and SR
> specific,
>    respectivly) discard counters exist under `errors-l3-rx` with
> no
>    corresponding traffic-accounting home for MPLS/SRv6-forwarded
>    packets. It would be nice to close structurally (a proper
>    address-family/sub-identity hierarchy).

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
Agree that there are other ways to model this. The reasoning for the approach in the spec is captured here:

   2.  There are many possible ways to define the discard classification
       tree.  For example, an approach is to use a multi-rooted tree,
       rooted in each protocol.  Instead, a better approach is to define
       a tree where protocol discards and causal discard classes are
       accounted for orthogonally.  This decision reduces the number of
       combinations of classes and has proven sufficient for determining
       mitigation actions.

More granular counters may be defined in the future if needed. The use of identities allows for extensibility. 

I don't think a change is needed.

issues.title: (Carlos Review) DF-driven discard
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 
> 3. `mtu-exceeded` does not distinguish DF-driven discard (IPv4
> DF=1,
>    IPv6 always) from fragmentable IPv4 (DF=0). As written it is
> one
>    undifferentiated leaf; the PMTUD/PLPMTUD-relevant distinction
> is
>    lost, and an implementer/operator cannot tell from the model
> whether
>    the packet was ever eligible for fragmentation. I can foresee
> many
>    automations that could depend on and use this (now missing)
>    information, both for RCA as well as for optimization.

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
add the sub classes
interface/discards/errors/l3/mtu-exceeded/fragmentation-not-permitted
interface/discards/errors/l3/mtu-exceeded/fragmentation-permitted-not-performed

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
interface/discards/errors/l3/mtu-exceeded/fragmentation-not-permitted
Reports packets discarded because the packet size exceeded the applicable MTU and the forwarding node was not permitted to fragment the packet. This includes IPv4 packets with the DF bit set and IPv6 packets, where fragmentation by transit nodes is not performed.

interface/discards/errors/l3/mtu-exceeded/fragmentation-permitted-not-performed
Reports packets discarded because the packet size exceeded the applicable MTU, fragmentation was permitted in principle, but the packet was not fragmented and forwarded. This includes IPv4 packets with the DF bit clear that were discarded due to local implementation behaviour, configuration, policy, resource limits, or other local conditions.

issues.title: (Carlos Review) discard taxonomy
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 4. The discard taxonomy (Section 4.2) is not fully 1:1 with the
> YANG
>    identities. Internet-area instances of this gap:
>    - GTSM (RFC 5082) hop-count-filtering drops are a control-plane
>      security classification, not ordinary TTL exhaustion in
> transit;
>      RFC 5082 Section 5 explicitly leaves silent-drop-vs-ICMP
> behavior
>      unspecified for non-matching packets, and RFC 6192 Section 4
> lists
>      GTSM alongside ingress filtering as a control-plane anti-
> spoofing
>      classification method. These could be classified under
>      control-plane `policy`, not `errors/ttl-expired`; the
> document does
>      not say so, and conflating the two counters makes both
>      operationally ambiguous. I recommend makign this explicit and
>      disambiguated.
>    - No subtype for IPv6 extension-header processing discards (RFC
> 8200
>      Section 4, RFC 9098).
>    - No subtype for reassembly failure (RFC 8200 Section 4.5, RFC
> 8900).
>    - TTL Expiry VCCV (RFC 5085 Section 5.1.3, CC Type 3) and MPLS
> LSP
>      Ping (RFC 8029) both deliberately set TTL to 1 to trigger OAM
>      interception at a target hop. That's intentional, not an
> error, and
>      `errors/ttl-expired` today has no way to tell the two apart.
> How to
>      disambiguate policy-driven from genuine transit exxhaustion?
> It's a
>      similar issue as the OAM-triggered TTL expiry and GTSM above.

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
add the following sub-classes

control-plane/discards/policy/gtsm

interface/discards/errors/l3/rx/ipv6-extension-header
interface/discards/errors/l3/rx/reassembly

interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/mpls-lsp-ping
interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/mpls-lsp-traceroute
interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/vccv

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
control-plane/discards/policy/gtsm
Reports control-plane packets discarded because they failed a Generalized TTL Security Mechanism (GTSM) check. These are packets addressed to a control-plane protocol session for which the received IP TTL or IPv6 Hop Limit did not satisfy the configured GTSM acceptance criteria. These packets are policy or security discards, not ordinary TTL-expired forwarding discards.

interface/discards/errors/l3/rx/ipv6-extension-header
Reports IPv6 packets discarded during IPv6 extension-header processing. This includes packets with malformed, unsupported, inconsistent, or otherwise invalid extension-header chains.

interface/discards/errors/l3/rx/reassembly
Reports packets discarded because fragment reassembly failed. This includes packets for which the required fragments were not received before the reassembly timeout, overlapping or inconsistent fragments were detected, or local reassembly resources were exhausted.

interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/mpls-lsp-ping
Reports TTL-expired packets that the implementation identifies as MPLS LSP Ping OAM packets. These packets are separated from ordinary transit TTL exhaustion because TTL expiry was associated with OAM processing rather than unintended packet lifetime expiry.

interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/mpls-lsp-traceroute
Reports TTL-expired packets that the implementation identifies as MPLS LSP traceroute OAM packets. This includes MPLS echo request packets using controlled TTL values to discover or test a specific hop along an LSP. These packets are separated from ordinary transit TTL exhaustion because TTL expiry is part of the OAM mechanism.

interface/discards/errors/l3/ttl-expired/oam/vccv
Reports TTL-expired packets that the implementation identifies as Virtual Circuit Connectivity Verification (VCCV) OAM packets using TTL expiry as the control-channel mechanism. This includes VCCV CC Type 3 packets where the PW label TTL is used to deliver the packet to the PE control or OAM function. These packets are separated from ordinary transit TTL exhaustion because TTL expiry is part of the OAM mechanism.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
**Authors agreement**:
* no need to be exhaustive here (see deployemnt section)
* these are intended discarded
* the classification may depend on where this happens duing internal processing. 
* Candidates for further discussion and usefulness exploratrion as part of a future augmentation of the module

issues.title: (Carlos Review) Nits
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 9. Section 1 scope reads "limited to reporting packet loss at
> Layer 3
>    and frames discarded at Layer 2" but MPLS/SRv6 discard reasons
> are
>    present throughout; please tighten the scope statement.
> 10. `invalid-vlan`/`invalid-mac` are Ethernet-specific; note
>     applicability for non-Ethernet L2 technologies.

issues.title: (Carlos Review) Minor Comments
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
> 5. `checksum-error` (errors-l3-rx) does not note that IPv4 has a
> header
>    checksum and IPv6 does not (RFC 791 vs. RFC 8200) --
> applicability by
>    address family could be stated.
> 6. `invalid-sid` lacks an informative reference to SRv6
> architecture
>    (RFC 8402 / RFC 8986).
> 7. No neighbor-resolution-failure (ARP/ND unresolved) discard
> subtype;
>    doesn't map cleanly to `no-route` or `internal`.
> 8. Foundational IP references (RFC 791, RFC 8200) are absent
> despite
>    heavy dependence on IP-layer semantics (TTL, header validity,
> MTU).
>    RFC 4884, RFC 5082, RFC 6192, RFC 5085, and RFC 8029 should
> also be
>    added as informative references if the comments are useful.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
> 8\. Foundational IP references (RFC 791, RFC 8200) are absent
>     despite

That's OK per the IESG statement on the matter: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/statement-iesg-iesg-statement-normative-and-informative-references-20060419/. Especially, this part

>  "It is not considered necessary to cite basic specifications that may be safely assumed to be known to practitioners (for example, [RFC 791](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc791/) need not be cited in every specification that mentions IPv4)."

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
> 5\. `checksum-error` (errors-l3-rx) does not note that IPv4 has a
>     header
>     checksum and IPv6 does not (RFC 791 vs. RFC 8200) --
>     applicability by
>     address family could be stated.

OK

> 6\. `invalid-sid` lacks an informative reference to SRv6
>     architecture
>     (RFC 8402 / RFC 8986).

OK

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
7. No neighbor-resolution-failure (ARP/ND unresolved) discard
subtype;
doesn't map cleanly to no-route or internal.

Add new sub-type:
interface/discards/errors/l3/neighbor-resolution-failure

leaf neighbor-resolution-failure {
  type yang:counter64;
  description
    "Reports packets discarded because the forwarding device
     could not resolve or use the link-layer adjacency required
     to forward the packet to the selected next hop.

     This includes packets dropped because ARP resolution for an
     IPv4 next hop failed, Neighbor Discovery resolution for an
     IPv6 next hop failed, or the resolved neighbor entry was
     unusable according to local neighbor state.

     This counter applies when route selection identified a next
     hop, but forwarding could not complete because the required
     neighbor or adjacency information was unavailable or unusable.
     Packets discarded because no route matched the destination
     are reported under 'no-route'. Packets discarded due to
     internal device faults are reported under 'internal'.";
}

issues.title: (Gorry DISCUSS)  congestion category
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
(1) To me it is unclear which category congestion-related loss of “normal”
traffic (e.g. BE) should be assigned. I was thinking that traffic bursts are
expected to result in some chance of packet discard at a router and that this
ought to be encouraged to trigger control for the transport congestion control
to yield effective utilisation. They are a necessary side effect of probing for
available capacity. I therefore suggest that some loss/AQM marks are not a
result of a configuration mistake, so to which category do these
congestion-related discards belong? Ought these to be a separate category
exactly to reveal more insight into where losses occur.

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
Proposed response:
For “normal” traffic (e.g. BE), discards would be assigned to interface/discards/no-buffer/qos/class(BE).  This may be classified as intended if it is below the operators SLA (e.g. loss rate per measurement interval), and unintended if above the operators SLA.  This example is in Appendix B.

issues.title: (Gorry DISCUSS)  AQM & loss: separate category?
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
(2) Then, AQM: It is good to encourage queues to configure AQM, but that also
results in discard that will be statistical as the endpoints seek to converge
on the available bottleneck capacity. These discards are not a configuration
problem, and seem entirely normal, at least expected during a busy hour,
although it would still seem to be helpful for an operator to know capacity is
being often reached (I suspect also they could also observable as the
utilisation by packet counters). Are we sure we wish to count these as
unintended, rather than in a separate category?

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
Suggested reply:
Where AQM drops packets, those should similarly be counted as congestive discards, not as errors; i.e. AQM discards would be assigned to interface/discards/no-buffer/qos/class(). This may be classified as intended if it is below the operators SLA, and unintended if above the operators SLA. This example is in Appendix B.

Where AQM marks packets, for example using ECN, those are not packet discards and are outside the discard counters, although they may be useful related signals for an operator.

We will add this case to the examples in Appendix B.

A1  AQM drop, <= SLA, unintended = N, no action
A2  AQM drop, > SLA, unintended = Y, move traffic or add capacity

issues.title: (Gorry DISCUSS)  Examples for class discards
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
(3) I agree that traffic policing/conditioning for QoS can result in discarded
that are not anticipated. e.g., this discard might be due to overload of a
provisioned EF service which the document correctly (in my reading) is an
intended packet discard. This might be clearly stated as an example?  For the
Lower than Best Effort (LBE) PHB, such loss seems acceptable/normal under
periods of high utilisation, so are these also intended?, and should this also
be explicitly said as an example ?

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
Proposed response:
If a configured policer drops traffic because the traffic exceeds a provisioned service profile, that is a policy discard: 
i.e., interface/discards/policy/qos/class(BE).

For example, EF traffic above its provisioned profile may be intentionally discarded by the policer. That does not mean the event is operationally irrelevant, because sustained discard may still indicate overload of the provisioned service or a mismatch between offered load and contracted capacity.

Similarly, for LBE traffic, loss during periods of high utilisation may be acceptable or intended according, subject to the operators SLA.

So to clarify: the model reports the discard cause, while intended versus unintended is determined by the operator using the discard class together with SLA, PHB, baseline, duration, scope, and service context.

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body:
It makes sense to add:
- an aqm sub-class
- policers per qos class

Hence, this is making me wonder if we should change the qos hierarchy, e.g.:

Option 1: closest to what we have
interface/discards/no-buffer/qos/class
interface/discards/aqm/qos/class
interface/discards/policy/qos/class/policer

Option 2: I think this is cleaner
|interface/discards/qos/class/no-buffer
|interface/discards/qos/class/no-buffer/aqm
|interface/discards/qos/class/policer

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
For consistency with no-buffer description in the doc, a simple approach would be to keep the current structure but graft aqm specifics as follows:

OLD:

>     |     +-- no-buffer
>     |        +-- qos!
>     |           +-- class* [id]
>     |              +-- id         string
>     |              +-- packets?   yang:counter64
>     |              +-- bytes?     yang:counter64

NEW:

>     |     +-- no-buffer
>     |        +-- qos!
>     |           +-- class* [id]
>     |              +-- id         string
>     |              +-- packets?   yang:counter64
>     |              +-- bytes?     yang:counter64
>     |              +-- aqm?
>     |                      +-- packets?   yang:counter64
>     |                      +-- bytes?     yang:counter64

Alternatively:

>     |     +-- no-buffer
>     |        +-- type* [drop-type]    <================ add a new identifty for no-buffer types: aqm, tail-drop.
>     |            +-- drop-type identityref
>     |            +-- qos!
>     |                 +-- class* [id]
>     |                 +-- id         string
>     |                 +-- packets?   yang:counter64
>     |                 +-- bytes?     yang:counter64

comments.author: o-pylypenko
comments.body: I think latter (drop-type) is best.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) ambigous checksum description
issues.author: boucadair

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: Already addressed with Carlos review.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) MTU Exceeded
issues.author: boucadair

comments.author: o-pylypenko
comments.body: I've moved mtu-exceeds from under /rx already in #91

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) invalid-sid
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
`leaf invalid-sid` it is unclear whether only routers whose address is the
destination address have to count or other routers in the path (which should
not check) also have to count.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: Only SR-aware routers will increment this when encountered.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) parity errors
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
`"The number of packets discarded due to parity errors."` does it mean that the
device CPU experimented a parity error during the receiving/forwarding of the
packet ? Please be specific.

comments.author: o-pylypenko
comments.body:
We can't: "These discards occur due to internal device issues, including: parity errors in device memory or other internal hardware errors"

This is intentionally generic -- every platform is different.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) ddos
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
I wonder how to ``"The number of packets discarded due to Distributed
Denial-of-Service (DDoS) protection policies."` as many DDoS are mitigated by
routing to a null-route or layer-3 ACL, which are different counters. Should it
be counted twice ?

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
We do already say: 

> In addition to the Layer 2 and Layer 3 aggregate classes, an individual discarded packet MUST only account against a single error, policy, or no-buffer discard subclass.

Added a bried clarification.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) L4 ACL
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
I am puzzled by the absence in the model and in the text about layer-4 ACL
support as they are often used. There should be at least some text in the
introduction and abstract about this limitation.

issues.title: (Eric DISCUSS) Encap
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
Probably due a misreading of mine, but I see no discard counters when
en/decapsulating a tunneled packet ? And/or how it can be differentiated from a
forwarding operation.

comments.author: o-pylypenko
comments.body:
I think we discussed it in the early stages and decided not to define dedicated encap/decap discard classes -- tunnel processing falls under forwarding, so when the required forwarding state is missing, semantically it is the same as no-route from an operator's perspective. Rule 13 also says that tunnel endpoint attribution relates to the outer header.


@johnevansuk

issues.title: (Ketan DISCUSS) MPLS
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
1) It is not clear to me whether this document considers MPLS traffic as L2 or
L3 (it is somewhat L2.5). Some aspects of L3 apply like "no route" but for MPLS
it should be "no ILM". Then again there is some coverage for SR-MPLS and
invalid-label under layer 3 so I am assuming it is considered under L3? In any
case, I was looking for some clarity and if discards for MPLS traffic would
benefit from their separate counters as opposed to putting them under L3. Under
L3, there is also address-family that covers IPv4 and IPv6 but not MPLS. There
are also cases where the MPLS label is disposed and inner IP packet processed
so we get multiple processing stages (see related point on double counting
further below). Same happens on the egress direction as well.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: see also #80

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: The discard is made bacause of a lockup op, hence the label discard case listed under l3 as well.

issues.title: (Ketan DISCUSS) double counting
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
2) Regarding the following text:

> 5. A frame accounted for at Layer 2 MUST NOT be accounted for at Layer 3 and
> vice versa. This is to avoid double counting.

I am assuming this is regarding discards. AFAIK packet reception would get
counted at both L2 and L3 layers? There are also some other similar
requirements for avoiding double counting which are not very clear to me. If we
take example of encapsulations (MPLS, IP-in-IP, etc.) then there can be
multiple layers of encapsulation that get disposed or imposed as the packet
traverses a router. How are they going to get accounted for in a manner that
avoids double counting?

issues.title: (Ketan DISCUSS)  order of discard
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
3) Regarding unclear normative MUST in the following text:

9, When there are multiple reasons for discarding a packet, the ordering of
discard class reporting MUST be defined. Typically, this can be exposed by an
implementation by means of discard-order-capability.

I am not sure what "MUST be defined" means. It sounds vague for something that
is a MUST.

issues.title: (Ketan DISCUSS) L2 qos
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
4) Regarding the following text, what about QoS based on IEEE 802.1Q PCP bits
or based on MPLS EXP bits ? What about QoS marking based on other than DSCP?

10. If Diffserv [RFC2475] is not used, no-buffer discards MUST be reported as
class[id="0"], which represents the default class.

issues.title: (Ketan DISCUSS) origin clarification
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
5) Could you clarify what is meant by "origin" here?

> 14. Traffic to the device control plane (to-CPU) has its own class. Traffic
> from the device control plane (from-CPU) is accounted for by origin,
> independent of the forwarding mechanism (e.g., any egress policer it
> traverses), and MUST also be accounted for in the same way as other egress
> traffic.
>

issues.title: (Chris DISCUSS) Documentation
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
"However, implementations MUST clearly document which features are supported
and how they map to the DM."

I'm wondering how anyone would really validate that?  This doesn't seem an
appropriate use of MUST to me.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: Let's s/MUST/must

comments.author: johnevansuk
comments.body: I'm sort of with Chris on this - I'm not sure this really means much and think we should just delete it

issues.title: Carlos Review: Add Foundational references
issues.author: johnevansuk
issues.body:
Add references:

8. Foundational IP references (RFC 791, RFC 8200) are absent despite
   heavy dependence on IP-layer semantics (TTL, header validity, MTU).
   RFC 4884, RFC 5082, RFC 6192, RFC 5085, and RFC 8029 should also be
   added as informative references if the comments are useful.

That's OK per the IESG statement on the matter: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/statement-iesg-iesg-statement-normative-and-informative-references-20060419/. Especially, this part

"It is not considered necessary to cite basic specifications that may be safely assumed to be known to practitioners (for example, RFC 791 need not be cited in every specification that mentions IPv4)."

That's OK for a generic protocol that runs over IP -- but that does not mean those citations ought to never be used. In this case, the keyword is "heavy dependence on IP-layer semantics". 

In either case, your choice if 791 and 8200 are needed -- but there was a second part to this comment:
" RFC 4884, RFC 5082, RFC 6192, RFC 5085, and RFC 8029 should also be ..."

Thanks!

Carlos.

issues.title: Gorry discuss: incorporate Gorry's examples
issues.author: johnevansuk
issues.body:
(1) To me it is unclear which category congestion-related loss of “normal”
traffic (e.g. BE) should be assigned. 

For “normal” traffic (e.g. BE), discards would be assigned to interface/discards/no-buffer/qos/class(BE). This may be classified as intended if it is below the operators SLA (e.g. loss rate per measurement interval), and unintended if above the operators SLA (e.g. loss rate in measured interval). This example is in Appendix B.

**That sounds good and I agree - if these two sentences are clear in the next revision, then this will have been resolved.** 

(2) Then, AQM: It is good to encourage queues to configure AQM, but that also
results in discard that will be statistical as the endpoints seek to converge
on the available bottleneck capacity. These discards are not a configuration
problem, and seem entirely normal, at least expected during a busy hour,
although it would still seem to be helpful for an operator to know capacity is
being often reached (I suspect also they could also observable as the
utilisation by packet counters). Are we sure we wish to count these as
unintended, rather than in a separate category?

Where AQM drops packets, those should similarly be counted as congestive discards, not as errors; i.e. AQM discards would be assigned to interface/discards/no-buffer/qos/class(). This may be classified as intended if it is below the operators SLA, and unintended if above the operators SLA. This example is in Appendix B.

Where AQM marks packets, for example using ECN, those are not packet discards and are outside the discard counters, although they may be useful related signals for an operator.

We have added an additional AQM discard type to clearly differentiate AQM discards from other congestive loss types: https://github.com/o-pylypenko/draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel/pull/104/changes#diff-6d4c7b48518a072a04219635c569dbd41793bae013f0df47f9cc908d7251f19f

**For me the no buffer "type" will address my primary concern here. Thanks for adding this.**

(3) I agree that traffic policing/conditioning for QoS can result in discarded
that are not anticipated. e.g., this discard might be due to overload of a
provisioned EF service which the document correctly (in my reading) is an
intended packet discard. This might be clearly stated as an example?  For the
Lower than Best Effort (LBE) PHB, such loss seems acceptable/normal under
periods of high utilisation, so are these also intended?, and should this also
be explicitly said as an example ?

If a configured policer drops traffic because the traffic exceeds a provisioned service profile, that is a policy discard:
i.e., interface/discards/policy/qos/class(BE).

For example, EF traffic above its provisioned profile may be intentionally discarded by the policer. That does not mean the event is operationally irrelevant, because sustained discard may still indicate overload of the provisioned service or a mismatch between offered load and contracted capacity.

Similarly, for LBE traffic, loss during periods of high utilisation may be acceptable or intended according, subject to the operators SLA.

**Exactly what I had hoped for. I anticipate the example will be clear in the new text, and this will be sufficient to address this.**

So to clarify: the model reports the discard cause, while intended versus unintended is determined by the operator using the discard class together with SLA, PHB, baseline, duration, scope, and service context.

issues.title: (Eric COMMENT) YANG for IM
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
### Section 4 (and its sub-section)

To be pendantic, I am really surprised (dare I say shocked) by the use of a
data modeling language to specify an information model rather than UML or ER
diagrams. See also RFC 3444.

Even more 'data model' flavour are the specific data types such as `type
yang:counter64;`, which clearly belongs to a data model and not an information
model.

Suggest renaming section 4 as a YANG *data* model (as well as changing the
title & abstract to remove 'information model').

If I was cynical, I would hint that it is more to have more implementations
having 'something' like the information while not implementing exactly the YANG
data model... But, this is only my personal feeling.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
The WG spent many cycles on this design aproach. 

Please note that draft-ietf-opsawg-rfc5706bis also says:

>    While an Information Model is typically described in English text (or
>    sometimes UML) to define the conceptual management requirements,
>    authors may choose to express it using YANG Data Structure Extensions
>    [RFC8791] as described in Section 5.3.1.  **Using YANG for the
>    Information Model can make it easier to link abstract concepts to
>    concrete data types in the corresponding Data Model, helping maintain
>    consistency between high-level design and practical deployment**.

issues.title: (Eric COMMENT) ttl /hop-limit
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
s/leaf ttl-expired/leaf hop-limit-expired/ after all we are in 2026.

`grouping errors-l2-tx ` would benefit from more leaves (e.g., interface down,
no carrier, exceeded transmission attemps, buffer full, ...).

Should something more specific about IPv6 extension headers be added in
addition of `leaf invalid-packet ` ?

While I am sure that some people will disagree with me (hence a COMMENT and not
a DISCUSS), `leaf invalid-label ` should be in errors-l2-rx as MPLS is more a
layer-2 (e.g., labels are valid only on a link) especially when a label is
flagged as 'invalid'.

comments.body:

See also the clarificaiton about the various use per address falmily in the draft.

reviews.author: boucadair
reviews.body:
as we are fixing nits. Here more :-)

Also, fixes the validation errors due to some references.

comments.body:
```suggestion
: The type or class of discards, which is crucial for selecting the appropriate mitigation. Examples may be:  error discards may require taking faulty components out of service, no-buffer discards may require traffic redistribution, or intended policy discards typically require no automated action.
```

```suggestion
While FEATURE-DISCARD-SCOPE, FEATURE-DISCARD-RATE, and FEATURE-DISCARD-DURATION are implicitly supported by the Interfaces Group MIB {{?RFC2863}} and the YANG Data Model for Interface Management {{?RFC8343}}, FEATURE-DISCARD-CLASS requires a more detailed classification scheme than they define. The Information Model provided in {{infomodel}} defines such a classification scheme to enable automated mapping from loss signals to appropriate mitigation actions.
```

```suggestion
The Information Model is defined using YANG {{!RFC7950}}, with Data Structure Extensions {{!RFC8791}}, allowing the model to remain abstract and decoupled from specific implementations in accordance with {{?RFC3444}}. This abstraction supports different data model implementations, such as YANG or IPFIX {{?RFC7011}}, while ensuring consistency across implementations. Using YANG for the Information Model enables this abstraction, leverages the community's familiarity with its syntax, and ensures lossless translation to the corresponding YANG data model, which is defined in {{datamodel}}.
```

we mix protocols vs. data model  (gNMI/SNMP)

```suggestion
:  These are discards due to buffer exhaustion (that is, congestion related discards). These can be tail-drop discards or due to an active queue management algorithm, such as Random Early Detection) (RED {{RED93}} or Controlled Delay (CoDel) {{?RFC8289}}.
```

```suggestion
This section is modeled after the template described in {{Section 3.7 of ?I-D.ietf-netmod-rfc8407bis}}.
```

pulls.title: Update draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel.md
pulls.author: johnevansuk
pulls.body: Updated data model differences from information per Benoit's feedback

comments.body:
```suggestion
This DM implements the IM defined in {{infomodel}} for the interface, device, and control-plane components. It is a device model per {{Section 2.1 of ?RFC8969}}. Specifically, it is a device-local (network element) operational state model: counters are scoped to a single device (interfaces and control plane). 

The IM defines the abstract classification tree using YANG data structure extensions {{?RFC8791}}. This DM imports that module and reuses the same groupings and hierarchy of components, directions, layers, and discard classes, attaching them via augment statements to existing YANG modules for routing, interfaces, and logical network elements. The flow component is defined only in the IM for use by flow-oriented data models and are not instantiated in this DM.
```

comments.body:
```suggestion
: These are intended discards, meaning packets dropped due to a configured policy, including: ACLs, traffic policers, unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF) checks, DDoS protection rules, and explicit null routes.  In practice, ingress DDoS protection policies are often realized using mechanisms such as ingress filtering and uRPF ({{?RFC2827}}, {{?RFC3704}}, {{?RFC8704}}), remotely triggered blackholing ({{?RFC3882}}, {{?RFC5635}}), or BGP Flow Specification–based filters ({{?RFC8955}}, {{?RFC8956}}, {{?RFC9117}}); all such policy-driven discards are reported under this class.
```

pulls.title: Addressing AD feedback
pulls.author: o-pylypenko
pulls.body:
Addressed comments:

* fixed TTL/RPF everywhere I could find
* fixed IANA namespaces
* added no-buffer to identities.
* clarified (hopefully!) control-plane direction semantics
* dropped gNMI reference -- it doesn't look to be referenced at all
* added a note wrt af extension
* minor diagram correction
* other nits like typos, L3 packets, RFC9907, and sub-types -> subtypes

rest:
* RFC 2475 is not a downref -- it is referenced informatively
* on zero-based-counter64: 1) Normally those counters are consumed as deltas over a polling interval, so initial value has no operational meaning, 2) zero on creation hence impose a constraint that is not needed and 3) ietf-interfaces does not [seem to use zero-based-counter64](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8343.html#section-3), and we're augmenting that.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
> * RFC 2475 is not a downref -- it is referenced informatively

This is needed normatively:

"If Diffserv [[RFC2475](https://o-pylypenko.github.io/draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel/draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel.html#RFC2475)] is not used, no-buffer discards MUST be reported as class[id="0"], which represents the default class."

I suggest to leave this as it is. No change needed.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
> * on zero-based-counter64: 1) Normally those counters are consumed as deltas over a polling interval, so initial value has no operational meaning, 2) zero on creation hence impose a constraint that is not needed and 3) ietf-interfaces does not [seem to use zero-based-counter64](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8343.html#section-3), and we're augmenting that.

Agree.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body: I agree that this is not needed but given how many may approach this spec, I think it is better to make the scope clear.

reviews.author: johnevansuk
reviews.body:
This feels unecessary:

Likewise, the document does not correlate specific discard counters with other events and counters (e.g., ICMP). Such a correlation is not trivial and depends on local configuration and specific conditions (e.g., rate-limit policy, overload, or Access Control Lists (ACLs)).

Suggested
This document considers only the signals that may trigger automated mitigation actions and not how those signals are processed or how the actions are defined or executed. These considerations are deployment-specific.

comments.body:
```suggestion
The scope of this document is limited to reporting Layer 3 packet discards, Layer 2 frame discards, and discards during MPLS label processing {{?RFC3031}}. The reported signals may trigger automated mitigation actions; the definition and execution of those actions are deployment-specific. Protocol actions associated with a discard (e.g., ICMP error message generation) are governed by the applicable protocol specifications and local policy.
```
agree with keeping follow-on protocol actions outside this model -- but could we tighten the paragraph like this?

- higher level checksum error for packets to/from the control plane (we don't have this but should add it under errored discards to the control plane)

comments.body:
https://github.com/o-pylypenko/draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel/pull/78#discussion_r3531628857

this statement needs a single place
This contradicts the amendment -- so probably need to drop the definition (intended/unintended) and just specifiy the mechanism? (policy/pipeline errors/etc)?

```suggestion
The classification defined in this document does not by itself determine whether a specific discard condition is intended or unintended. That determination is made by the operator, based on local policy, configured intent, baseline behavior, duration, affected scope, service context, and other operational evidence. For example, policy discards may be intended when they enforce a deliberate access-control rule, but unintended when a configuration error causes valid traffic to match that rule. Similarly, TTL-expired packets may be expected at a low baseline rate due to traceroute or other diagnostic activity, while a sustained increase above baseline may indicate convergence, a routing loop, or another operational fault.
```
Sorry Oleks - not sure what you mean?
Yes you're right - we need to delete all of the "These are intended/unintended discards" - will address separately
I mean that this clarification in more or less same form is at least here, then further down on line 174, and proposed in #78 line 111.
ack - removed

reviews.body: Looks good to me - thanks Oleks!

pulls.title: Johnevansuk patch 11
pulls.author: johnevansuk
pulls.body:
Address Carlos comments:
5. `checksum-error` (errors-l3-rx) does not note that IPv4 has a header checksum and IPv6 does not (RFC 791 vs. RFC 8200) -- applicability by address family could be stated.
6. `invalid-sid` lacks an informative reference to SRv6 architecture (RFC 8402 / RFC 8986).

Changes
- Clarify that checksum-error reports IPv4 header checksum failures only.
- State that checksum-error is not applicable to IPv6 internet-layer header processing.
- Clarify that upper-layer checksum failures are outside this L3 checksum-error counter.
- Clarify invalid-sid as SRv6 SID processing failure.

comments.body: duplicated description -- fails yanglint
nit: I think listing all possible reasons is not required, just "can't be processed ... " is descriptive enough.
Fixed thanks Oleks

pulls.title: Update ietf-packet-discard-reporting-common.yang
pulls.author: johnevansuk
pulls.body:
Addressed Carlos comments:

10. `invalid-vlan`/`invalid-mac` are Ethernet-specific; note applicability for non-Ethernet L2 technologies.

pulls.title: Add MTU fragmentation discard subclasses
pulls.author: o-pylypenko
pulls.body:
Refs #81

Add an aggregate `packets` counter:
- `fragmentation-not-permitted` for IPv4 packets with DF set and IPv6 packets; and
- `fragmentation-permitted-not-performed` for fragmentable IPv4 packets that were discarded without being fragmented.

comments.body:
```suggestion
   : These discards occur when the packet size exceeds the applicable MTU. The subclasses distinguish whether the forwarding node was permitted to fragment the packet ({{Sections 3.1 and 3.2 of !RFC791}}, {{Sections 4.5 and 5 of !RFC8200}}).
```

pulls.title: Update draft-ietf-opsawg-discardmodel.md
pulls.author: johnevansuk
pulls.body:
To address Gorry's feedback, have refactored:
-Determining Intent for Policy section to
-Determining Operator Intent
Broadened the description and added other examples

comments.body:
```suggestion
Some discard classes provide a strong signal on their own. For example, errors/l2/rx above baseline will generally indicate unintended loss, since it reports errored received frames. Similarly, TTL-expired packets may be expected at a low baseline rate due to traceroute or other diagnostic activity, while a sustained increase above baseline may indicate convergence issue, an infinite routing loop, or another operational fault.
```

```suggestion
Congestion-related loss depends on operator context. A level of no-buffer discards below a defined traffic performance indicator(captured in an SLA, typically) may be expected or intended. The same discard class above a performance indicator, sustained for longer than expected, may be unintended and thus require action.
```

```suggestion
Policy discards may also require additional context. Discards reported under policy (for example, ACL or policer discards) indicate only that traffic matched a configured rule. They may still be unintended if the configuration is in error. Determining intent for policy discards requires operator-local context, such as configuration validation, service policy, and change history. Operators should verify configuration enforced in underlying nodes and continousily ensure that configuration is consistent with the intended service to deliver.
```
Config validation is how we try and prevent this, but if it happens in operation (i.e. has slipped through config validation) then its correlated the unexpected increase in ACL discards with a change
"class above the SLA" is meaningless to me John.
Yeah. The point is to remind what is already done here. Thiis an OPS considerations section after all :-)
That's what I mean - operationally we deal with this by correlated the unexpected increase in ACL discards with a change
